Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Accession AT4G25050 is explicitly listed in Materials/Methods and Accession numbers for AtACP4.

Source_Species.reason: The study is on Arabidopsis, explicitly given throughout the paper.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly stated in the abstract/materials.

Applied_Species.reason: Mutants and overexpression lines were tested in Arabidopsis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is explicitly the experimental species.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is explicitly used as the wild-type background/control.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is the Arabidopsis wild-type background used in the study.

Enzyme_Name.reason: ACYL CARRIER PROTEIN4 / AtACP4 is explicitly named; 'Acyl carrier protein 4' is the direct expansion.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: ACP4 is explicitly described as an ACP isoform, supporting membership in the acyl carrier protein family.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results show ACP4 affects MGDG, PG, SQDG, and DGDG molecular species/content.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: These terminal products are glycerolipids/chloroplast membrane lipids discussed throughout the paper.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: The affected lipids are described as chloroplast/photosynthetic membrane lipids.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: Abstract and Results state ACP4 acts in de novo fatty acid biosynthesis and affects chloroplast lipid assembly/biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper supports ACP4 affecting acyl-ACP pools and PA/DAG precursor supply for MGDG and PG, i.e. the chloroplast precursor-supply branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: ACP4 is presented as affecting the acyl-ACP pool upstream of PA and DAG formation.

Primary_Substrate.reason: ACP4 is described as a scaffold/substrate carrier in fatty acid biosynthesis and as interacting with acyl-ACP-utilizing enzymes such as LPAT/SAD; this supports acyl intermediates on ACP and acyl-ACP donor pool wording.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper directly links ACP4 to the size/profile of the acyl-ACP pool that supports PA/DAG precursor formation.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Although ACP4 is not a catalytic enzyme in the classic sense, the description matches the paper's stated role as the major plastid acyl carrier/scaffold supplying acyl-ACP substrates for chloroplast PA synthesis.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: ACP4 is linked to end products by supplying precursor pools rather than a terminal enzymatic conversion.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results support ACP4 maintaining acyl-ACP pools and affecting PA precursor formation, with consequences for chloroplast lipid biosynthesis, especially MGDG and PG.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point is supported: ACP4 is the major leaf ACP isoform; acp4 causes ~90% drop in total acyl-ACP; acp4 affects chloroplast lipids including MGDG/PG/SQDG/DGDG; ACP4 interacts with LPAT and SAD; ACP4 overexpression does not rescue rbl10.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed approaches are explicitly used in Results/Methods: T-DNA mutants, double mutants, overexpression, acyl-ACP profiling, lipidomics, membrane yeast 2-hybrid.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Abstract states ACP4 is the most abundant ACP isoform in Arabidopsis leaves.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The same statement applies to the tested Arabidopsis leaves.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Current paper supports direct interaction with LPAT and SAD by yeast 2-hybrid; RBL10 interaction is referenced from prior work and also mentioned in this paper's framing. Thus overall list is supported.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: ACP4 is discussed in the context of plastid/chloroplast fatty acid and lipid biosynthesis.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Growth conditions and acetate [14C] feeding assays in isolated chloroplasts are explicitly described.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The cautious wording matches the paper: ACP4 is not formally shown rate-limiting, but is shown to be a major determinant of acyl-ACP pool size/composition.

Omics_Data.reason: LC-MS/MS lipidomics and LC-MS/MS acyl-ACP analyses are explicitly described; no accession/deposition is reported.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The mechanistic basis is supported, but explicit breeding/engineering application value is not discussed in the paper; this is an extrapolative interpretation.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: acp4 mutants are described as pale green, smaller, with >2-fold lower chlorophyll and reduced total photosynthetic membrane lipids.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Results support reduced plastid C16 contribution, increased ER compensation/36:x species in some pools, and ACP4 overexpression causing increased C18:3 and reduced C16:3 in MGDG/PG.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: AT1G25290 is explicitly listed under Accession numbers for AtRBL10.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Arabidopsis thaliana is the experimental species.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Col-0 is the Arabidopsis background used.

Enzyme_Name.reason: RBL10 is explicitly referred to as plastid RHOMBOID LIKE10; 'Rhomboid-like protein 10' is faithful.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: RBL10 is explicitly a rhomboid-like protein; membrane association is consistent with the paper's description.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The strongest supported final products are MGDG and PG, both analyzed in Results and Conclusion as modulated by RBL10.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: MGDG and PG are glycerolipids/chloroplast membrane lipids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: These are chloroplast/photosynthetic membrane lipids according to the paper.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The abstract and Results support RBL10 affecting conversion of chloroplast PA to DAG precursors linked to galactolipid biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The paper specifically places RBL10 at the chloroplast PA-to-DAG branch for MGDG biosynthesis.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: RBL10 is described as acting on PA turnover/conversion to DAG, a midstream precursor-conversion step.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Results repeatedly identify increased 34:x PA in rbl10, supporting 34:x PA as the affected substrate pool.

Primary_Product.reason: Decreased 34:x DAG in rbl10 chloroplasts supports 34:x DAG as the immediate affected product pool for MGDG biosynthesis.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The cautious wording matches the paper: RBL10 appears to promote turnover/conversion of chloroplast-derived 34:x PA into DAG for MGDG, while direct biochemical activity is unresolved.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: RBL10 is linked to end products through formation of the immediate precursor DAG.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results support PA accumulation, reduced 34:x DAG, and a shift toward ER-derived galactolipid species in rbl10.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All listed points are supported by Fig. 4/Fig. 6 and accompanying text: 34:x PA accumulation, reduced 34:x DAG/C16:3-MGDG, slower PA turnover to MGDG, shift of MGDG/DGDG toward 36:x species, and partial compensation in acp4 rbl10.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: T-DNA mutant analysis, double mutant analysis, lipidomics, and isolated chloroplast acetate [14C] feeding assays are all explicitly used.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: ACP4 interaction with RBL10 is explicitly mentioned as a previous finding and motivates this study.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: RBL10 is explicitly designated plastid RHOMBOID LIKE10.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Growth conditions and acetate [14C] chloroplast feeding assays are explicitly stated.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The cautious statement aligns with the paper: RBL10 is suggested to be important for PA turnover to DAG/MGDG, but formal rate-limiting status is not established.

Omics_Data.reason: LC-MS/MS lipidomics is explicitly described; no accession is reported.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not explicitly discuss engineering applications; this field is an extrapolation from the results.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results support altered chloroplast lipid composition in rbl10, including reduced C16:3-MGDG and decreased MGDG/PG ratio.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states that direct biochemical activity remains elusive and proposes possible roles in PA turnover/flipping or related mechanisms rather than confirmed proteolysis.
